dominant_error_pattern: Student confuses organizational roles: treating APA and NASP as interchangeable or misattributing regulatory/credentialing authority when NASP (not APA) credentials school psychologists and oversees the NCSP.
instructional_red_flags: Distractors B and C blur APA and NASP functions. Teach the distinction: NASP credentialsschool psychologists via NCSP; APA serves broader psychology. Use visual: Credential flows (NASP → NCSP) vs. organizational membership (APA or NASP are independent). Have students map which organization makes which decisions.

dominant_error_pattern: Student misinterprets NASP's position on homeschooling as oppositional or risk-focused, missing that NASP's stance emphasizes partnership and support rather than skepticism.
instructional_red_flags: Distractors A and C frame homeschooling negatively. Remedy: Share NASP position statement on homeschooling directly. Teach: NASP sees homeschooled families as partners; assessment tools are valid with this population; collaboration + support is the model. Provide example: A school psychologist consultation with homeschooling parent on reading screening, without gatekeeping.

dominant_error_pattern: Student shortcuts the proficiency pathway by either relying on formal training alone or skipping supervision, missing that competent test administration requires both knowledge transfer and supervised practice.
instructional_red_flags: Distractor A overvalues course completion; Distractor D undervalues supervision. Teach the three-step sequence: (1) Formal training/study (manual, examples), (2) Practice with feedback (supervised administrations), (3) Proficiency check-in (supervisor sign-off). Use rubric: Can the clinician detect when a student is uncomfortable? Can they adapt rapport while maintaining standardization? Supervised practice reveals gaps a video cannot.
